data.title: Westwood outlines $450M new client mandate and ETF growth amid $1B AUM outflows
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Westwood Holdings Group, Inc. (WHG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Casey highlighted several milestones, stating the ETF franchise now exceeds $200 million, including the launch of the Enhanced Income Opportunity ETF, while MDST surpassed $170 million in AUM. He noted, "We closed our second oversubscribed private equity fund, Westwood Energy Secondaries Fund II with more than $300 million in commitments for the fund and two related co-investment funds. Our managed investment solutions team secured its first institutional client, and we had strong full year sales growth. $2.5 billion versus $2.1 billion, up 20%."
  • Casey reported, "Institutional channel achieved gross sales growth of 36% versus the previous year," with particular traction in SMidCap and SmallCap value strategies.
  • He emphasized product expansion, sharing, "The infrastructure and liquid real asset strategies we launched last year have attracted strong interest from institutional investors seeking alternatives to traditional equity and fixed income allocations."
  • On the wealth division, Casey described a "multiyear evolution" focused on scalability and client experience, and expressed, "We remain focused on enhancing the client experience, improving scalability and positioning our business for sustainable long-term growth."
  • On ETF progress, Casey stated, "Our MDST ETF has now achieved the asset scale required for approval on major broker-dealer platforms and we expect new platform additions this year."
  • CFO Murray Forbes reported, "Today, we reported total revenues of $27.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $24.3 million in the third quarter and $25.6 million in the prior year's fourth quarter."
  • Forbes added, "Our fourth quarter income of $1.9 million or $0.21 per share compared to the third quarter's $3.7 million or $0.41 per share due to higher performance-related incentive compensation in the fourth quarter and unrealized depreciation on strategic private investment in the third quarter, offset by higher revenues."

Outlook

  • Casey projected ongoing "market uncertainty driven by a variety of economic indicators and policy developments," but maintained confidence that Westwood's "focus on high-quality businesses with strong fundamentals positions us well for the future."
  • He revealed, "We did have a new client come in yesterday with $200 million, and they will add another $100 million to $200 million over the next couple of months. We also have a new defined contribution plan that will fund on the last day of the first quarter in our SMid product for $450 million."
  • Casey said, "Our pipeline looks great. We have -- we reached another new threshold last night where MDST our midstream enhanced energy income fund crossed the $200 million threshold."

Financial Results

  • Forbes stated, "For fiscal 2025, total revenues of $97.8 million compared to $94.7 million in 2024 driven by higher average assets under management and higher revenues from our ETFs and private energy secondaries funds."
  • Forbes detailed, "Economic earnings for the year were $14.3 million or $1.61 per share compared with $7 million or $0.82 per share in 2024."
  • Firm-wide assets under management and advisement totaled $17.4 billion at quarter end, consisting of $16.5 billion in AUM and $0.9 billion in assets under advisement.
  • Forbes explained, "Over the year, our assets under management experienced net outflows of $1 billion and market appreciation of $1 billion and our assets under advisement experienced net outflows of $18 million."
  • Forbes reported, "Our financial position continues to be very solid, with cash and liquid investments at quarter end totaling $44.1 million and a debt-free balance sheet."
  • Casey disclosed, "Our Board of Directors approved a regular cash dividend of $0.15 per common share payable on April 1, 2026."

Q&A

  • No analyst questions were recorded in the Q&A session. The operator closed the Q&A, returning the call to Casey, who addressed outflows and the outlook: "We felt like we had a really good year in 2025, but we want to acknowledge the outflows in the fourth quarter, which were disappointing. More than 80% of the outflows were from our large-cap value product...most of those large cap outflows, in fact, more than 80% of those flows were from one sub-advisory client that carries a fee of less than 20 basis points."
  • Casey followed, "We did have a new client come in yesterday with $200 million, and they will add another $100 million to $200 million over the next couple of months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts did not participate in the Q&A, so their sentiment was not captured.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in prepared remarks, but acknowledged disappointment regarding outflows. Casey said, "We felt like we had a really good year in 2025, but we want to acknowledge the outflows in the fourth quarter, which were disappointing."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic with a focus on growth, to a more measured optimism coupled with candid recognition of outflows and their limited revenue impact.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased to $27.1 million in Q4 from $24.3 million in Q3, while income dropped to $1.9 million from $3.7 million, attributed to higher performance-related incentive compensation.
  • Assets under management decreased to $16.5 billion from $17.3 billion, with net outflows of $1 billion in Q4 versus $0.7 billion in Q3.
  • Management highlighted strong pipeline prospects, including a $450 million mandate, and continued ETF growth, consistent with previous quarter commentary but with greater emphasis on addressing outflows.
  • The previous quarter’s call noted disappointment in outflows, but this quarter provided additional detail about their source and revenue impact, reflecting a more transparent approach.
  • Analyst participation was lower this quarter compared to the previous quarter’s Q&A.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "outflows in the fourth quarter," particularly from a large-cap value product, as a key concern, but emphasized the low fee impact on revenue.
  • Casey indicated efforts to mitigate outflows by pointing to new client wins and funding commitments, as well as a robust pipeline and anticipated platform additions for ETFs.

Final Takeaway

Westwood Holdings Group closed 2025 with notable growth in its ETF franchise, strong institutional sales, and new fund launches, while addressing a challenging quarter characterized by significant outflows from a large-cap value product. Management underscored the lower revenue impact of these outflows and pointed to robust pipeline opportunities, including a $450 million SMid product mandate and further ETF platform expansion. The company remains confident in its active management strategy and distribution momentum, supported by a solid balance sheet and continued dividend payments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Agnico Eagle plans 20%–30% decade-long production growth as cash flow, reserves reach record highs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ammar Al-Joundi highlighted, "In 2025, as gold prices hit new highs throughout the year, Agnico Eagle delivered on our production targets, we delivered on our costs, and we did it responsibly and reliably." He emphasized that cash costs increased only $76 per ounce compared to a $1,700 rise in the gold price, stating, "We delivered over 95% of this gold price increase to the benefit of our shareholders, delivering on our core mandate of providing gold upside leverage to our owners."
  • Al-Joundi reported, "We repaid almost $1 billion in debt. We built up almost $3 billion in cash, and we returned over $1.4 billion directly to our owners through dividends and share buybacks, all while continuing to invest heavily in our future through the largest exploration budget we've ever had and through continued strong investments into our 5 key growth projects."
  • The CEO revealed Agnico Eagle's plan to increase production by up to 20% to 30% over the next decade, with a "path to over 4 million ounces of annual production by the early 2030s."
  • Projects at Detour Lake, Canadian Malartic, Upper Beaver, and Hope Bay are being accelerated, with significant capital increases and earlier production targets. For instance, investment at Detour Lake is tripling to $300 million, aiming for underground production as early as 2028, and Upper Beaver investment is rising to $300 million to support production by 2030.
  • CFO James Porter stated, "We finished the year with a solid fourth quarter, producing approximately 841,000 ounces of gold at total cash cost of $1,089 and all-in sustaining costs of $1,517 per ounce."
  • Porter reported, "We delivered a number of financial records in the fourth quarter, including record adjusted earnings of approximately $1.4 billion or $2.70 per share and record free cash flow of over $1.3 billion or $2.62 per share."
  • Porter also announced, "We increased the quarterly dividend by 12.5% to $0.45 per share. And at current gold prices, we expect to be more active on share buybacks. To support this, we intend to renew our normal course issuer bid in May and increase the purchase limit up to $2 billion."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a stable annual production profile between 3.3 million and 3.5 million ounces over the next three years, with 2026 cash costs forecast to be up a little over $100 per ounce compared to last year, largely due to higher royalties and a stronger Canadian dollar.
  • Porter said, "The midpoint of our 2026 guidance ranges are $1,070 per ounce for cash costs and $1,475 per ounce for all-in sustaining costs. Approximately 60% of the increase in cash costs relative to 2025 reflects higher royalties, driven by a higher budgeted gold price of $4,500 per ounce and the impact of a stronger Canadian dollar."
  • Porter outlined, "We see the potential to increase [shareholder returns] to 40% or higher this year with flexibility depending on the gold price and the needs of the business."
  • The company expects to maintain capital expenditures at an elevated level as it accelerates growth projects, with Hope Bay potentially adding $300 million to 2026 capex if approved in May.

Financial Results

  • Porter reported, "For the full 2025 year, we exceeded the midpoint of our guidance with gold production of 3.45 million ounces."
  • Total cash costs and all-in sustaining costs for 2025 were $979 and $1,339 per ounce, respectively, both slightly above guidance due to higher royalty costs. He noted, "If we exclude the impact of higher royalties, our total cash cost would have been $937 per ounce, $42 per ounce lower and below the midpoint of our guidance."
  • Agnico Eagle reported record free cash flow of approximately $4.4 billion for the year, repaid approximately $950 million in debt, and ended 2025 with $2.9 billion in cash.
  • Shareholder returns through share buybacks and dividends totaled $500 million in Q4 and a record $1.4 billion for 2025.

Q&A

  • Lawson Winder, BofA Securities, Research Division: Asked about M&A activity and whether Agnico would tender shares to an offer on Foran. CEO Al-Joundi responded, "That's not really something I would be comfortable discussing," and elaborated that the company's focus for M&A is always on value per share, stating, "We are willing to move and we have moved when we see an opportunity on the M&A side that actually creates value per share."
  • Fahad Tariq, Jefferies: Inquired about cost productivity initiatives and their inclusion in 2026 ASIC guidance. Dominique Girard replied, "It's partially included, but not all...we keep some flexibility in that."
  • Joshua Wolfson, RBC Capital Markets: Questioned future capex trends with project ramp-ups. Porter said, "We will see capital kind of stay at that elevated level. And then once we start to see that stair step increase in production in 2030, you'd expect the capital to start to come off."
  • Daniel Major, UBS: Asked about cost estimates for Meadowbank life extension ounces. Girard confirmed, "The last number I saw was sort of in the $2,200 to $2,300" (ASIC).
  • Anita Soni, CIBC: Queried dividend policy sensitivity to gold price changes. Porter responded, "The reality is the gold price could pretty well be cut in half, and we'd be okay maintaining that level of dividend."
  • Tanya Jakusconek, Scotiabank: Sought confirmation of capital needs for project growth through 2031. Porter estimated, "$5 billion to $6 billion of growth spending over the course of '26 through 2030, I think is about the right estimate."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on capital allocation, project execution, and cost inflation, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, frequently congratulating the company on results but seeking clarity on guidance and capital returns.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently using phrases such as "We are confident..." and "We are exceptionally well positioned."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from an emphasis on productivity and cost control to more probing questions on capex, M&A, and capital returns, while management's confidence increased, supported by record results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management unveiled a decade-long growth vision targeting over 4 million ounces of annual production by the early 2030s, a notable forward shift from Q3’s focus on ongoing project execution.
  • Dividend growth and share buyback expansion were announced, contrasting with Q3’s commentary on maintaining capital returns.
  • Capex guidance increased with more aggressive project acceleration, while Q3 focused on optimization and productivity improvements.
  • Analyst focus this quarter shifted toward long-term growth, capex needs, and capital allocation, versus prior quarter’s emphasis on productivity and near-term cost control.
  • Management’s tone was more assertive and optimistic, citing record cash, reserves, and resources, and outlining bolder growth targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged higher costs for 2026, primarily due to royalties and currency impacts, and highlighted ongoing inflationary pressures on labor and consumables, with labor at "about 45% of our overall cost structure."
  • The company faces elevated capex as it accelerates key growth projects, with risk mitigated through staged investments and maintaining a strong balance sheet.
  • Analysts raised questions about technical and human capital constraints as project activity ramps up, but management expressed comfort with current capacity and flexibility to adjust project timelines.

Final Takeaway

Agnico Eagle delivered on its 2025 commitments with record production, reserves, cash flow, and shareholder returns, while laying out an ambitious plan to grow production by up to 30% over the next decade. Management signaled continued discipline in capital allocation, a willingness to accelerate high-return projects, and a focus on delivering value per share. The company stands in its strongest financial position ever and expects to maintain industry-leading costs and robust shareholder returns as it pursues its next phase of growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines 'willing to move' on deals if opportunities emerge, CEO says
data.description:
Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) is "very well-positioned" to pursue acquisitions if the right opportunity emerges, CEO Ammar Al-Joundi said Friday, signaling a renewed openness to dealmaking after a longtime focus on raising production from existing mines.

"We are willing to move... when we see an opportunity on the M&A side that actually creates value per share," the CEO said on Agnico Eagle's (AEM) earnings conference call, according to Bloomberg, adding that the company has a "very good understanding of the various assets out there."

Al-Joundi told Bloomberg last year that the company was focused on internal growth and warned about "irresponsible M&A" just because of high gold prices, but on Friday's call, the CEO said assets with strong exploration potential would offer the most high-value opportunities.

"What would really interest us - and what has really driven us for external M&A - has really been exploration upside," Al-Joundi said.

Agnico Eagle (AEM) closed +5.5% on Friday after reporting Q4 adjusted earnings and revenues that topped analyst estimates, and raising its quarterly dividend by 12.5% to $0.45/share, which the company said reflects the strength of the business and higher gold price environment.

Gold production of 841K oz for Q4 and 3.45M oz for the full year met company guidance, but costs were higher than expected, with Q4 all-in sustaining costs of $1,517/oz and full-year AISC of $1,339/oz exceeding the top end of guidance, largely due to higher royalty expenses tied to stronger realized gold prices.

Agnico Eagle's (AEM) year-end gold mineral reserves rose 2.1% to a record 55.4M oz, supported by exploration success and the initial declaration of reserves at the Marban deposit in Malartic following the March 2025 acquisition of O3 Mining.

data.title: Farmer Bros. anticipates gross margin relief in Q4 2026 as green coffee costs ease
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Farmer Bros. Co. (FARM) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO John Moore stated that the quarter was more challenging for Farmer Brothers, citing “pressure across our results due to higher cost inventory flowing through our COGS and continued micro and macroeconomic pressures.” He highlighted year-over-year improvement in selling and general and administrative costs, with gross margin maintained above 35%, and reiterated the company’s expectation for gross margin to remain above this level for the remainder of fiscal 2026.
  • Moore noted, “our adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was only slightly positive at approximately $0.5 million.” He reported a decrease in total coffee pounds of just under 3% and a 1% decrease in net sales to $88.9 million compared to the prior year period.
  • Moore emphasized continued commitment to driving top line revenue growth and addressing declines in customer counts and coffee pounds. He highlighted leveraging the DSD network, growing the white label portfolio, and better utilizing the Portland roasting facility as key strategies.
  • Moore also referenced recent reductions in tariff rates and declines in Arabica and Robusta markets, stating that if these trends continue, “we expect to start seeing some improvement in gross margins beginning in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026 with further benefits in fiscal 2027.”
  • He confirmed that the strategic committee remains active in assessing potential opportunities to maximize shareholder value and will provide updates when appropriate.
  • CFO Vance Fisher reported, “adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $484,000 compared to $5.9 million in the prior year period.” Fisher also stated, “gross margin was 36.3%, a 680 basis point decline compared to the prior year period.” He added, “overall, year-to-date gross margin was in line with our expectation at 38%.”
  • Fisher reported that operating costs for the quarter decreased $1.4 million to $36.4 million, including decreases in both selling and G&A expenses mainly due to reduced personnel-related costs.

Outlook

  • The company expects gross margins to remain pressured and average in the high 30s for the fiscal year, but anticipates some relief starting in the fourth quarter due to recent declines in the green coffee market.
  • Moore stated, “we anticipate it [gross margin] will stay for the remainder of the fiscal 2026,” and Fisher reiterated, “we do anticipate some relief starting in the fourth quarter due to recent declines in the green coffee market.”
  • No explicit changes in guidance metrics or new guidance ranges were provided compared to the prior quarter.

Financial Results

  • Net sales during the quarter were $88.9 million, down 1% compared to $90 million in the prior year period.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $484,000 compared to $5.9 million in the prior year period.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 36.3%, a 680 basis point decline compared to the prior year period; year-to-date gross margin was 38%.
  • Operating costs for the quarter were $36.4 million, a decrease of $1.4 million from the prior year period.
  • Net loss was $4.9 million in the second quarter compared to $200,000 of net income in the prior year period.
  • As of December 31, 2025, the company had $4.2 million of unrestricted cash and cash equivalents and $24.6 million available under its revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: The transcript does not include a Q&A section or any analyst questions or management responses.

Sentiment Analysis

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: The transcript does not include any analyst commentary or Q&A.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was slightly cautious yet maintained confidence, with Moore stating, “we remain confident we are on the right path.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained consistent, balancing acknowledgment of ongoing challenges with a focus on operational discipline and future margin improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company continues to report declining coffee pounds and net sales, though the rate of decline appears to have moderated compared to the prior quarter.
  • Gross margin declined further, dropping to 36.3% from the previous quarter’s 39.7%, reflecting the anticipated impact of green coffee inflation.
  • Operating cost reductions continued, with a $1.4 million decrease this quarter following a $4.5 million decrease in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s guidance language and strategic priorities remain focused on cost control, margin preservation, and leveraging core operational assets such as the DSD network and Portland facility.
  • There was no Q&A in either quarter, so no shift in analyst focus or tone can be assessed.

Risks and Concerns

  • Moore cited “continued micro and macroeconomic pressures” and referenced industry reports indicating declining U.S. consumer confidence and purchasing power.
  • The company continues to face pressure from higher cost inventory and commodity price volatility, though recent declines in coffee commodity prices and tariffs may offer future relief.
  • Management’s mitigation strategies include cost controls, leveraging the DSD network, expanding the white label portfolio, and optimizing facility utilization.

Final Takeaway

Management reported a challenging quarter driven by higher costs and softening demand, but highlighted continued gross margin discipline and cost reductions. While year-over-year sales and coffee volumes declined, Farmer Bros. remains focused on unlocking value through its DSD network, white label growth, and operational efficiencies. The company expects gross margin pressure to persist through fiscal 2026, with potential improvement in the fourth quarter if current commodity trends hold. Strategic initiatives and ongoing evaluation of shareholder value opportunities remain priorities for the back half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fortune Brands outlines $3.35-$3.65 EPS guidance for 2026 and $35M cost savings plan as CEO transition announced
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fortune Brands Innovations (FBIN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Susan Kilsby announced a leadership transition, with Amit Banati appointed as the new CEO effective in May, following Nick Fink’s departure. Kilsby stated, "Succession planning is something we talk about at the Board on an ongoing basis... we are extremely fortunate and excited to appoint Amit Banati as our new Chief Executive Officer."
  • CEO Nicholas Fink addressed significant profitability headwinds in 2025, citing "significant volume deleverage, high single digits, which created intense pressure on profitability, particularly in the fourth quarter." Fink detailed a "comprehensive profitability reset," which included a 10% reduction in headquarters workforce and $60 million in savings. Further, Fink announced operating income savings initiatives of $35 million annualized run rate by year-end 2026, not yet included in 2026 guidance.
  • Fink highlighted market performance: "Based on our point-of-sale data, we estimate that, excluding China, we outperformed the market for our products by approximately 130 basis points for the full year and approximately 300 basis points in the fourth quarter."
  • CFO Jonathan Baksht reported, "For the full year, total company sales were $4.5 billion, down 3%, excluding the impact of China, sales were down 1%." Baksht added, "Consolidated operating income was $699 million, down 10% and operating margin was 15.7%, down 120 basis points. Earnings per share were $3.61, down 12%."

Outlook

  • The company provided 2026 guidance for net sales growth of "approximately flat to 2%" and operating income margin of "approximately 14.5% to 15.5%." EPS is expected to be "approximately $3.35 to $3.65," and free cash flow is projected at "$400 million to $450 million in 2026." The $35 million in annualized operating income savings is not included in the 2026 guidance, but management confirmed it will be at run-rate by year-end.
  • Management stated, "Our outlook for 2026 does not include a near-term demand inflection or a recovery from current levels."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter sales were $1.1 billion, down 2%. Excluding China, sales were flat. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $0.86, down 12%.
  • Water segment Q4 sales were $617 million, down 4%, with operating income of $141 million and margin of 22.8%. House of Rohl delivered "low double-digit net sales growth." Moen experienced "low single digits" decline due to wholesalers managing inventory. Flo achieved "double-digit growth."
  • Outdoors Q4 sales were $295 million, down 3%. Operating income was $42 million with margin of 14.2%. LARSON achieved share gains, while Fiberon lost business with a key retailer.
  • Security Q4 sales were $166 million, up 6%. Operating income was $22 million, up 52% with margin of 13.4%. Yale saw "double-digit growth."
  • Free cash flow for the full year was $367 million. Net debt at year-end stood at $2.3 billion with net debt to EBITDA of 2.6x.

Q&A

  • John Lovallo, UBS: Asked about margin compression in 2026. Jonathan Baksht responded, "as we start rolling over some of the tariff impact and under absorption from our balance sheet into the P&L into the first half of the year, you are going to see some margin compression... on a mitigated basis, we're looking at about $151 million of tariff impacts in 2026, so an increase of just over $100 million year-over-year."
  • Philip Ng, Jefferies: Queried on market growth assumptions and outgrowth versus the market. Nicholas Fink stated, "we decided as we built the model that we wanted to model a year for 2026 that essentially looks like 2025 without an inflection and without an improvement." Baksht added, "we have a unique set of market... correlation model... probably need some refinement."
  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays: Asked about Water segment guidance and margin impacts from commodities. Baksht said, "we're looking at roughly $40 million of impact for commodity inflation from our cost of goods sold. I would say about just under half of that is in the Water segment."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: Questioned the timing of CEO transition and possible portfolio or personnel changes. Fink explained the transition follows "3 phases of transformation... now building momentum behind execution." Kilsby confirmed, "There is nothing planned at this time" regarding personnel changes.
  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: Inquired about digital portfolio growth and margin declines in Water and Outdoors. Fink reported, "Flo growth in excess of 50% for the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern regarding margin compression, tariff impacts, and leadership transition, with questions focusing on the specifics of cost pressures and the sustainability of market outperformance. The tone was slightly negative, reflecting skepticism about near-term recovery and profitability improvements, especially in Outdoors.
  • Management maintained a measured and transparent tone, frequently acknowledging headwinds and dissatisfaction with current profitability. Fink stated, "we are not satisfied with our profitability today." CFO Baksht's tone was factual and emphasized mitigation efforts and ongoing reviews of cost structure. Compared to the prior quarter, management was more defensive and explicit about ongoing challenges and restructuring needs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a major leadership change with the announcement of a new CEO, marking a significant shift from the previous quarter’s focus on operational transformation.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more defensive posture, explicitly acknowledging dissatisfaction with margins and profitability, and communicating new cost-saving initiatives not previously detailed.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more conservative, explicitly stating no expected market inflection, compared to last quarter’s reference to potential flat market and outperformance. Analysts in the current quarter pressed harder on margin and cost concerns, while last quarter saw broader questions on strategy and growth.
  • Key metrics such as operating margin and EPS have trended lower versus previous quarter’s results and guidance, with explicit references to increased tariff and cost pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, continued volume deleverage, and significant tariff headwinds as primary risks.
  • There is explicit acknowledgment of underperformance in certain product lines, especially in Outdoors and Fiberon, and the need to restore momentum.
  • Analyst concerns focused on the sustainability of cost controls, the potential for further margin compression, and the timeline for recovery in key segments.
  • The company has not included anticipated cost savings from new initiatives in the 2026 outlook, which could present upside risk if executed effectively but also suggests continued caution.

Final Takeaway

Fortune Brands Innovations is entering a period of leadership transition and intensified cost discipline as it faces persistent market and profitability challenges. The company is guiding for flat to modest sales growth and EPS of $3.35 to $3.65 in 2026, with significant cost reduction initiatives underway but not yet factored into guidance. Management has openly acknowledged ongoing macro headwinds, margin pressures from tariffs and inflation, and the need for further operational efficiency, while also highlighting outperformance in select segments and the digital portfolio. The new CEO is expected to sustain the transformation momentum, with near-term results highly dependent on successful execution of cost controls and improvement initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Soros Fund ups stake in Microsoft, NVIDIA, Apple as part of Q4 moves
data.description:
Hungarian-American investor George Soros's Soros Fund increased its stake in Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Apple (AAPL) as part of its Q4 portfolio positioning changes.

The global asset manager and family office added ~161K shares of Microsoft's common stock, ~118K shares of NVIDIA's common stock, and ~66K shares of Apple's common stock to its portfolio.

Other notable stake increases were made in Exelon (EXC) (~488K shares), Electronic Arts (EA) (~318K shares), Atlassian (TEAM) (~216K class A shares), Uber Technologies (UBER) (~119K shares), and Salesforce (CRM) (~55K shares), the fund disclosed in its latest 13F filing.

Meanwhile, Soros Fund cut its holdings in Snowflake (SNOW) by ~168K shares, Circle Internet Group (CRCL) by ~151K class A shares, and Interactive Brokers (IBKR) by ~813K shares.

During the quarter, the fund initiated a position in New Gold (NGD), DigitalBridge (DBRG), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), Exact Sciences (EXAS), and Xcel Energy (XEL), among others. It exited KeyCorp (KEY), CareTrust REIT (CTRE), Cipher Mining (CIFR), and KKR & Co. (KKR).

data.title: Mercer signals $120M mass timber revenue for 2026 while advancing cost reduction targets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mercer International (MERC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Juan Bueno stated that operating results "continue to be undermined by significant market headwinds" and noted the recognition of noncash asset impairments, including a major write-down at the Peace River mill. He emphasized that underlying operational performance improved quarter-over-quarter, with cost reductions and efficiency improvements contributing to a $54 million increase in liquidity. Bueno highlighted the "One Goal One Hundred" program, which delivered about $30 million in cost savings for 2025, and reaffirmed the target of $100 million in improvements by the end of 2026.
  • Bueno reported progress on the carbon capture demonstration unit at the Peace River mill with Svante Technologies: "the pilot plant is operating and the results so far are very encouraging, both in terms of the efficiency as well as purity of the CO2 being captured."
  • Bueno described a "healthy order book" for the mass timber business, with the order backlog at $163 million compared to $80 million at the end of Q3, and projected 2026 mass timber revenue of about $120 million.
  • CFO Richard Short stated, "our operating EBITDA for the fourth quarter was negative $20 million, up $8 million when compared to the third quarter's results." He reported a $216 million noncash impairment charge, primarily linked to Peace River, and a $12 million impairment in the solid wood segment. Short also noted, "in the fourth quarter, our aggregate liquidity improved by over $54 million to $430 million, comprised of about $187 million of cash and $243 million of undrawn revolvers."

Outlook

  • Management expects "modest NBSK and NBHK price improvements in Q1 in both Europe and China, while North America is expected to be stable."
  • Bueno projected 2026 mass timber revenue "to be about $120 million," with expectations that "revenues to be more than double what we had in 2025 or above $120 million."
  • Planned capital expenditures for 2026 are forecasted at $60 million to $80 million, focused on maintenance, environmental, and safety projects.
  • The company targets "almost 50 days less planned maintenance downtime compared to 2025."

Financial Results

  • Mercer reported a consolidated net loss of $309 million for Q4 or $4.61 per share, including $239 million in noncash impairments, compared to a net loss of $81 million or $1.21 per share in Q3.
  • Operating EBITDA for Q4 was negative $20 million, representing an $8 million improvement from Q3.
  • Pulp sales volumes increased by 20,000 tons to 472,000 tons, while pulp production was stable at 460,000 tons.
  • Mass timber revenues grew roughly 6% compared to Q3, and the order book reached $163 million at quarter end.
  • Aggregate liquidity rose by $54 million to $430 million.

Q&A

  • Roger Spitz, BofA Securities, asked about maintenance covenant headroom and working capital outflow; Short responded, "we're comfortable that we're well under the covenants at the end of the quarter, but we will expect them to get tighter as the year progresses... It will be a net outflow probably around $100 million to $150 million at this point."
  • Sean Steuart, TD Cowen, inquired about asset recycling and potential closures; Bueno replied, "the possibility of claiming reasonable value for any particular asset in this cycle is not the right time. But we are obviously looking into that as we focus on our core assets and see our way through this trough."
  • Edward Brucker, Barclays, questioned the future of Peace River; Bueno stated, "what we're working on is our transition from less hardwood to more softwood. That is center and foremost... by the end of this year, we expect it to be 50-50."
  • Cole Hathorn, Jefferies, explored fiber cost inflation in Germany; Bueno detailed how German energy policy and reduced harvesting have increased wood chip costs, adding, "that's why we're expecting fiber prices to increase until we see proper harvest levels come back and after we get through the seasonal aspect of bio pellets and biofuels."
  • Dominic Gerster, Aperture Investors, asked about RCF extensions; Short said, "the conversations are going well... before the end of Q2 for sure."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised persistent concerns about liquidity, asset sales, and cost inflation, maintaining a neutral-to-cautious tone, with repeated questions on working capital outflow and asset strategies.
  • Management maintained a measured, slightly defensive tone when discussing impairments and cost pressures but was more confident regarding growth in mass timber and cost savings, as Bueno stated, "we're confident in this business being a growth engine for Mercer in the short term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited slightly more caution due to large impairment charges and ongoing market headwinds, but optimism was higher around mass timber and cost control.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net loss increased sharply due to noncash impairments in Q4, while EBITDA loss improved by $8 million from Q3.
  • Liquidity position improved by $54 million in Q4 versus Q3, attributed to working capital management and cost reductions.
  • Mass timber backlog doubled quarter-over-quarter, with management projecting significant revenue growth in 2026.
  • Management’s tone in Q4 was more focused on liquidity and asset rebalancing, while Q3 emphasized initial progress on cost structure improvements and the early stages of the One Goal One Hundred program.
  • Analysts’ questions remained focused on liquidity and asset strategy, but showed heightened concern about working capital outflow and large impairment charges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing market headwinds, pricing weakness, and elevated fiber costs in Germany and Canada as significant challenges.
  • Trade uncertainty and tariffs remain major overhangs, especially with upcoming CUSMA negotiations.
  • Management expects fiber costs to "increase meaningfully for both our pulp and sawmill businesses" in Q1 2026.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the potential for further asset impairments, working capital outflows, and the timing of asset sales or closures.

Final Takeaway

Management is navigating a challenging market marked by continued pricing weakness, rising fiber costs, and significant noncash asset impairments, most notably at the Peace River mill. Despite these headwinds, the company improved its liquidity through active cost and working capital management, advanced its One Goal One Hundred cost reduction program, and reported robust growth in its mass timber business, projecting revenues above $120 million for 2026. The company remains focused on liquidity, prudent capital allocation, and strategic transformation toward diversified, sustainable revenue streams amid persistent industry uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amwell outlines path to cash flow breakeven in Q4 2026 with high-quality revenue focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Well Corporation (AMWL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ido Schoenberg, Chairman & CEO, described 2025 as a “pivotal year” marked by a major transformation and a sharpened focus on offering what he called “the best tech platform in the market.” He stated, “We divested non-core activities. The sale of APC is one example. We restructured our company and dramatically reduced our cost base. We realigned our road map and go-to-market investments.” Schoenberg emphasized that 2026 begins with “clear visibility towards the goal of cash flow breakeven from operations in Q4.”
  • Schoenberg highlighted the company’s strategic shift, saying, “Our 2026 top line is smaller, but now it's primarily high-quality, high upside, sticky revenue. This gives us clear visibility to reach our cash flow breakeven goal in Q4 of this year.” He noted that Amwell is now “building deeper, long-term relationships with the payers, government and health systems.”
  • Mark Hirschhorn, Executive VP, COO & CFO, reported, “Total revenue for the year was $249.3 million. Importantly, subscription revenue continued to become a larger and more durable component of our business, representing 53% of total revenue, up from 45% in 2024.”
  • Hirschhorn also emphasized cost control and profitability progress: “We reduced both net loss and adjusted EBITDA losses by approximately $100 million each, driven by disciplined cost actions and a more focused operating model.”

Outlook

  • Hirschhorn provided guidance for 2026: “For the full year 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $195 million to $205 million. We expect AMG visits between 1.32 million and 1.37 million visits. Adjusted EBITDA loss in the range of $24 million to $18 million.” For Q1 2026, “we expect revenue in the range of $48 million to $53 million, and an adjusted EBITDA loss in the range of $7 million to $5 million.”
  • Hirschhorn stated, “Based on our current outlook and continued execution, we expect the company to achieve positive cash flow from operations in the fourth quarter of this year.”

Financial Results

  • Hirschhorn reported, “Total revenue in the quarter was $55.3 million, representing a 22.1% year-over-year decline. Subscription revenue was $28.8 million, down 22% year-over-year.” He attributed the decline to “the step down in our DHA contract this past summer, churn that occurred earlier in 2024 and to a lesser extent, our reprioritization of certain parts of the business to focus on our core payer and government markets.”
  • Amwell Medical Group visit revenue was $23.7 million. Paid AMG visits were flat at approximately 340,000 visits in the quarter. Total platform visits were 1 million, down 28.4% year-over-year.
  • Cost of goods sold was $27 million, resulting in gross profit of $28.3 million. Gross margin was 51.2%, down 280 basis points year-over-year. Operating expenses, including depreciation and amortization, were $55.3 million, a 30.7% reduction year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was a loss of $10.3 million, improving from a loss of $12.7 million in the previous quarter. Net loss was $25.2 million compared to $30.7 million in the third quarter.
  • Cash burn for the quarter was approximately $19 million, with $182 million in cash and marketable securities and no debt at year-end.

Q&A

  • Corey DeVito, Wells Fargo Securities: Asked about contract renewal timelines and government opportunities. Schoenberg responded, “In 2025, we signed 50 contracts, most of which are renewals… The amount of what we renewed in '26 are significantly lower with one important exception, which is the DHA renewal that we expect to have this summer.”
  • Jailendra Singh, Truist Securities: Inquired about the competitive landscape with new AI entrants. Schoenberg answered, “The integration of those AI programs and the ability to integrate, switch and maintain multiple AI programs turned out to be a very big challenge for our customers… our platform would be an important utility as our clients do that.”
  • David Larsen, BTIG: Asked about headwinds, tailwinds, and specifics on the DHA renewal. Hirschhorn said, “The most likely tailwind would be an earlier adoption of our technology-enabled platform… We do, of course, have a renewal with the DHA this summer. We're extremely confident that, that will be renewed again and hopefully for a longer term.”
  • Craig Hettenbach, Morgan Stanley: Queried about long-term growth prospects with a smaller, higher quality revenue base. Schoenberg highlighted, “We believe that the same-store growth presents a very meaningful revenue opportunity for us… the government sector represents an incredible growth opportunity as well.”
  • Eric Percher, Nephron Research: Asked about revenue quality and divestitures. Schoenberg replied, “We divested APC, and we deemphasized other areas that are non-core. Essentially, our one product right now is still a very good match to all the market segments…”
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham & Company: Sought insight on renewal discussions informing go-to-market strategy. Schoenberg shared, “All of our renewals… were related to the same offering to the new Amwell Platform. And in many ways, while they are technically renewals… you can consider them in many ways, a new sale or almost a net new sale.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on renewal risks, adoption of AI, and long-term growth clarity. The tone was generally neutral, with some probing on competitive threats and contract renewals.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, repeatedly referencing cost controls, subscription durability, and “clear visibility” to cash flow breakeven. Hirschhorn stressed, “We expect the company to achieve positive cash flow from operations in the fourth quarter of this year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted to greater confidence and clarity about the company’s direction, whereas analyst questions remained consistently focused on core business risks and long-term growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management further streamline operations, complete divestitures, and shift to a single core platform offering. This contrasts with the previous quarter’s emphasis on broad product integration and early-stage divestitures.
  • Guidance for 2026 was more explicit, with management forecasting revenue between $195 million and $205 million and a target for positive cash flow in Q4, compared to previous guidance focused on cost containment and broad operational efficiency.
  • Analyst focus remained on contract renewals, particularly the DHA, and the long-term impacts of AI integration, similar to the prior quarter.
  • Margins and operating expenses continued to improve, with a more significant drop in operating expenses this quarter.
  • Management’s confidence in hitting cash flow breakeven has grown, and there is a clearer articulation of the strategic shift to high-quality, recurring revenue.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued churn, the need to renew the DHA contract, and the challenge of integrating rapidly evolving AI programs as ongoing risks.
  • Hirschhorn noted the potential revenue impact if the DHA contract is not renewed or expanded, stating that adding back previously eliminated digital behavioral health and automated care programs to the DHA contract would be “material.”
  • Analysts pressed on potential headwinds to guidance, competitive threats from new AI entrants, and clarity around revenue quality as non-core assets are exited.
  • Management responded by emphasizing the high renewal rate, the shift to a single platform, and ongoing cost discipline as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Amwell’s leadership emphasized that the company enters 2026 with a streamlined business model centered on a single, high-quality technology platform, a strong recurring revenue base, and no debt. Management stated that strategic divestitures and a focus on core payer and government markets position the company to achieve cash flow breakeven from operations in the fourth quarter of 2026, while continuing to invest in AI-driven innovation, deepen client relationships, and pursue meaningful growth opportunities in both same-store and government sectors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bridgewater Associates adds new stakes in CAT, DELL, exits T, NLY, among Q4 moves
data.description:
Bridgewater Associates, the hedge fund manager founded by billionaire investor Ray Dalio, took new positions in Caterpillar (CAT), with about 62.2K shares, Dell Technologies (DELL), with 156.3K shares, and Spotify Technology (SPOT), with 23.5K shares, during Q4 2025, according to a U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filing released on Friday.

Conversely, the firm closed out its holdings in AT&T (T), Annaly Capital Management (NLY), Affirm Holdings (AFRM) and Aehr test Systems (AEHR.

Increased stakes include Nvidia (NVDA), to 3.87M shares in Q4 from 2.51M shares in the prior quarter, Amazon (AMZN), to 1.95M from 1.12M, and Mastercard (MA), to 409.2K from 366.3K, the 13F filing showed.

Trimmed stakes: Salesforce (CRM), to 1.93M shares in Q4 from 2.01M in Q3, Microsoft (MSFT), to 984K from 1.10M, and Wells Fargo (WFC), to 1.14M from 2.54M.

data.title: China's supercharged gold trading looks like 'speculative bubble,' Capital Economics says
data.description:
China's recent gold trading frenzy appears more like a speculative bubble than a typical flight to safe-haven assets, raising the risk of more market turbulence, Capital Economics analysts said Friday.

"The growing use of leverage and futures trading to gain exposure to gold in China suggests that the recent increase in Chinese gold demand is more consistent with a speculative bubble inflating," analyst Hamad Hussain said in a note.

Private Chinese investor demand is a major driver of gold prices, fueling greater use of gold-linked financial products, and the increase in gold warrants on the Shanghai Futures Exchange also indicates that futures trading has become a bigger part of China's gold market, Hussain explained.

The continuing property downturn should support safe-haven demand for gold in China in the medium term, Hussain added, but he also noted that Chinese investors are increasingly using leverage to gain exposure to gold, which could boost market volatility and the likelihood of sharp price swings like those seen earlier this year.

Gold and silver prices jumped on Friday, as weaker-than-expected U.S. inflation results reignited hopes for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year, offsetting concerns from stronger-than-expected jobs data earlier in the week.

"The broader backdrop remains one of elevated volatility following this week's sharp liquidation across precious metals, but today's move suggests the correction may have overshot, with bargain hunting and position adjustments now providing support," ING commodity strategist Ewa Manthey said in a note.

On Friday, front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed +2% to $5,022.00/oz, and front-month Comex February silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +3% to $77.851/oz.

Gold and silver both rose 1.4% this week; gold has gained in eight out of the past 10 weeks, and silver has finished higher in 11 out of the past 15 weeks.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
